title: Peasants Under Siege: The Collectivization of Romanian Agriculture, 1949-1962 By Gail Kligman and Katherine Verdery Princeton University Press. 2011. 520 pages. $95 cloth, $39.50 paper
abstract: Kligman and Verdery draw on decades of ethnographic fieldwork in Romania as well as on research conducted by a team of scholars in the early 2000s using archives and interviews, to examine how the Communist Party imposed agricultural collectivization on a reluctant peasantry between 1948 and 1962. Although one might assume that a 500-page tome would address nearly every question that could be asked, this volume narrows its scope by focusing on the Party's efforts to create cadres, the “pedagogy” of cadre interaction with peasants – especially the significance of violence in promoting collectivization – and the reasons for the varied pace of collectivization in the 11 communities where interviews were conducted. Peasants Under Seige is explicitly comparative, drawing on the vast scholarly literature on Soviet and other Communist experiments with collectivization, to demonstrate both common patterns and aspects unique to Romania. The authors comment: “For social science, there is nothing new in observing that blueprints rarely conform to how they are implemented, whether the location be the Soviet bloc, postsocialist transformation…. More interesting is to ask how the effort to impose the model spurred improvisation and ­innovation….”(448) Kligman and Verdery assert that Romania's Communist Party, which was very small when the Soviets empowered them, chose collectivization under heavy Soviet pressure, and that the process of collectivization was at the same time a process for building a huge and thoroughly embedded Communist Party. Although Romania's Communists copied a Soviet model, and continually referred to the Soviet example, Romania's agricultural economy was based on family smallholdings and had no pre-existing communal structures to draw on.
